title: F1 in Belgium: Machine learning algorithms are ruining the sport
gist: The 2026 F1 hybrid ruleset gives cars so little battery energy (4 MJ) that drivers coast through high-speed corners to recharge, and opaque self-learning AI energy-deployment systems now produce unexplained car-to-car performance gaps, degrading the on-track product at marquee venues like Spa.

themes.theme: Hybrid ruleset guts the racing product

insights.claim: The 2026 Formula 1 battery can power the car's electric motor for only a few seconds per lap, leaving cars energy-starved with less rear-wheel power than the junior F2 feeder series for most of a lap.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A rules regime that makes the premier series slower than its feeder undermines the entertainment value underpinning F1's media-rights and sponsorship economics.
insights.quote: a 2026 F1 battery is able to power the race car’s electric motor for only a few seconds a lap; for much of the rest of the lap, the energy-starved cars have less power going to their rear wheels than in the junior F2 feeder series.
insights.locator: As we saw at Suzuka in Japan earlier this year

insights.claim: Because the 2026 hybrid units provide so little energy, going flat out is no longer the fastest way to complete a lap, so drivers now coast into once-committed high-speed corners to divert engine power to recharge the battery.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: The ruleset structurally penalizes the exact high-speed circuits (Spa, Suzuka, Monza) that draw the biggest crowds and TV audiences.
insights.locator: Not this year. As I’ve detailed more than a few times now

insights.claim: In sector two at Spa, 2026 F1 cars run on the engine alone at roughly 450-500 bhp—about Formula 3 power levels but with F1 downforce—per driver Max Verstappen.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Grid-driver disillusionment is a leading indicator of talent flight and fan attrition that regulators may be forced to reverse.
insights.quote: For most of sector two, you run just on the engine. So what is that? 450 bhp, 500 bhp? Something like that, which is, I guess, more or less what a Formula 3 car has but with F1 downforce.
insights.locator: “For most of sector two, you run just on the engine

themes.theme: Opaque AI energy management as a hidden failure mode

insights.claim: Teams now use self-learning AI systems to manage per-lap energy deployment, and their opacity to the driver makes them the increasingly likely cause of unexplained straight-line speed deficits between identical cars.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Illustrates how black-box ML in high-stakes systems can create performance losses that operators cannot diagnose in real time—a broader cautionary signal for AI deployment.
insights.quote: The self-learning AI systems that teams are now using to manage their energy deployment strategies across a lap are looking increasingly likely to blame. By their nature, they’re fairly opaque to the driver.
insights.locator: The self-learning AI systems that teams are now using

insights.claim: With only 4 MJ of battery capacity, tiny unplanned energy deployments—from filling in torque or a bit of wheelspin—can throw off the whole energy-management model and leave a driver with a fraction of expected power.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Extreme sensitivity to small inputs makes the systems brittle, a design lesson for any tightly-constrained autonomous control system.
insights.quote: even tiny deployments to fill in extra torque to help the V6 or from getting a bit of wheelspin is enough to throw off the energy management and leave a driver with a fraction of the power they thought they should have.
insights.locator: And because the electrical energy is so limited

insights.claim: George Russell reported watching his steering wheel show declining speed while at full throttle on the straight and being unable to explain or fix it, with his team unable to solve the deficit before the race.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A driver declared champion-in-waiting being beaten by an unexplained system fault shows how technology, not talent, is now decisive—reframing where teams should invest.
insights.quote: you’re watching on your steering wheel, [you’re] losing speed when you’re full gas on the straight. You feel powerless. We don’t know what’s going on.
insights.locator: “Even my last lap, for some reason

insights.claim: Max Verstappen could activate an escape clause in his Red Bull contract because he sits outside the top two of the drivers' championship, with a move to McLaren or a full F1 sabbatical rumored as alternatives.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A Verstappen exit would reshape team valuations and sponsorship flows; the trigger is contractual and championship-position based, so watch the standings.
insights.locator: Rumors continue to swirl about Verstappen’s future

insights.claim: Teenager Kimi Antonelli notched his sixth win of the season at Spa and leads his Mercedes teammate George Russell by two full race wins in the standings.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Antonelli's dominance signals adaptability to the new cars is the key competitive variable this season.
insights.locator: The race was won, as so many others this season, by Antonelli
